paper: Global uniqueness for an inverse boundary problem arising in elasticity
structure_note: Standard math paper structure: Summary, Introduction with statement of results (section 0), one technical section establishing the diagonalization machinery (section 1), one technical section proving the main theorem (section 2), References.

sections.title: 0. Introduction and statement of the results
sections.role: Sets up the elastic medium, defines the Dirichlet-to-Neumann (DN) map for the elasticity system, states the main theorem (global uniqueness of Lame parameters from the DN map in n>=3), and sketches the strategy: diagonalize the elasticity operator, build exponentially growing solutions, reduce to hyperbolic Cauchy problems, conclude lambda1=lambda2 and mu1=mu2.

sections.title: 1. Complete diagonalization
sections.role: The technical heart. Reduces the elasticity operator (a second-order system) to a pseudodifferential system whose principal part is diagonal, then to a first-order system that can be FULLY diagonalized modulo smoothing. This is the new machinery the paper introduces. Constructs the exponentially growing solutions (Calderon-style, but for elasticity, not conductivity) that the next section feeds into.

sections.title: 2. Proof of Theorem 0.7
sections.role: Closes the proof. Substitutes the special exponential solutions into the integral identity (Proposition 0.9), takes limits as the complex frequency goes to infinity, and reduces the problem to a pair of strictly hyperbolic PDEs in (lambda1-lambda2) and (mu1-mu2). Uses uniqueness of the Cauchy problem for hyperbolic operators (starting from the boundary, where the parameters already agree thanks to Theorem 0.8) to propagate the equality throughout the domain. Conclusion: lambda1=lambda2 and mu1=mu2 in all of Omega.
